title: Revisiting Hand of Fate: Ordeals - A Game that Should have been a Massive Hit
body:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DAuagmdRSOE

Daniel (@dungeondive) revisits Hand of Fate: Ordeals and makes a passionate case: this should have been a massive hit. It's the deck-builder that most feels like a fantasy adventure game — better at it than Clank or Thunderstone Quest, whose praise he doesn't get. The art by Ian O'Toole still floors him: bold, evocative, wood-carving-like, lending the whole thing a tarot-relic atmosphere, and there's a ton of it. Building a character by slotting weapons and armour onto your card, then binding attacks to your weapon, genuinely feels like outfitting a hero.

His real focus this time is Endless mode (in the special edition), which he argues should have been called Roguelike mode — and may be the best roguelike experience he's had on the tabletop. You shuffle all locations into one seeded world, every location single-use and mandatory, with loitering penalties pushing constant exploration and a burn token endlessly cycling the shop. Beat a boss, advance a level, reset harder, repeat until death. With both the publisher and the video-game studio now gone, no more is coming — but he says there's more than enough game in the box. A heartfelt re-recommendation.

----

Daniel calls Hand of Fate: Ordeals' Endless mode one of the best tabletop roguelikes he's played, yet it flopped and the studio's gone. Why do you think genuinely good games sometimes fail to find their audience?
